question_title: Will the nationwide #1 party’s vote share in the Netherlands municipal elections be at least 15.0% ?
summary:
The 2026 Dutch municipal elections (March 18) have a strong downward trend in top party vote share: 15.7% (2010) → 14.4% (2014) → 13.5% (2018) → 11.7% (2022). Only 1 of 4 recent elections exceeded the 15% threshold.

The main path to YES is the GroenLinks-PvdA combined entity on verkiezingsuitslagen.nl. Their combined 2022 total was 17.8%, and 88% of dual-presence municipalities run combined lists in 2026. However, the formal merger isn't complete until June 2026 (AFTER the election), Amsterdam runs separate GL/PvdA lists, and the combined entity captures only ~80% of total GL+PvdA votes, yielding a median of ~13.2%. The secondary path via D66 (surging to ~19% nationally) requires breaking their historical municipal underperformance pattern. Monte Carlo simulation under the dominant split-reporting scenario gives P(YES) ≈ 21%, with a small upside from the possibility of consolidated reporting.

factors.description: Strong downward trend in top party vote share: 15.7% (2010) → 14.4% (2014) → 13.5% (2018) → 11.7% (2022). Only 1/4 recent elections exceeded 15%.
GL-PvdA combined lists in 88% of dual-presence municipalities create a large entity on verkiezingsuitslagen.nl, concentrating ~80% of total GL+PvdA votes.
Formal merger not complete until June 2026 (AFTER March election). Amsterdam runs separate GL/PvdA lists. This limits combined entity's capture rate to ~80%.
D66 national surge to ~19% creates secondary path, but historical municipal/national ratio of 0.6-0.77 makes 15% municipal unlikely.
GL+PvdA total municipal vote likely 15-17% (down from 17.8% in 2022 due to D66 drain). Combined entity needs 18.5%+ total to reach 15%, which is above most recent baselines.
Local parties stable at ~30% of municipal vote, compressing national party shares and making 15% threshold harder.

reasoning:
**Forecast: 28% probability (logit: -0.94)**

Key reasoning:

- **Historical trend strongly against YES**: Top party share has declined from 15.7% to 11.7% over four election cycles. Only 2010 exceeded 15%.
- **GL-PvdA merger creates a plausible path**: Their combined 2022 total was 17.8%, and 88% of municipalities run combined lists. But the formal merger isn't complete until June 2026 (after the March election), so verkiezingsuitslagen.nl will report split entities.
- **Amsterdam running separate lists** is critical - it's the largest municipality and heavily left-leaning, reducing the combined entity's capture rate to ~80%.
- **D66 surge** is a secondary path but requires breaking historical municipal underperformance patterns.
- **Monte Carlo result**: ~21% under dominant split-reporting scenario, boosted to ~28% by small probability of consolidated reporting.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the nationwide #1 party’s vote share in the Netherlands municipal elections be at least 15.0% ?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/42115)
[Opinion polling for the 2025 Dutch general election - Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opinion_polling_for_the_2025_Dutch_general_election)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves as “Yes” if the official nationwide results for the [municipal elections](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Dutch_municipal_elections) show that the party with the highest nationwide vote share has a vote share ≥ 15.0%, as reported before May 1, 2026. Use the nationwide (landelijk) results table on Verkiezingsuitslagen.nl as the primary source: [https://www.verkiezingsuitslagen.nl/.](https://www.verkiezingsuitslagen.nl/.) The question may also resolve based on [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions).
fine_print:
Question resolves based on the nationwide vote share (percentage of valid votes) for the party ranked #1 nationwide by vote share.

This question resolves based on reporting by Verkiezingsuitslagen.nl. Therefore, parties will be treated exactly as presented by the resolution source and Metaculus will not merge or separate entities beyond what the source presents.

For purposes of this question, "party" refers to an individual national political party or electoral joint list. Thus, aggregated categories that combine the votes of multiple distinct local parties (e.g., *Lokale partijen*) will not be treated as a merged single national party.
background_info:
This forecasts how fragmented the national vote is in the 2026 municipal elections, using the top party’s nationwide vote share as a compact measure of concentration.
